---
title: "Orthogonality and Least Squares"
---

## Role

This page is the bridge from geometric linear algebra to practical estimation and optimization.

## Why It Matters

Least squares is one of the cleanest places to see geometry, computation, and application meet.

## Intuition

When `Ax = b` has no exact solution, the next best thing is to find the vector `x` whose image `Ax` gets as close as possible to `b`.

That “closest point” idea becomes a projection problem.

## Formal Core

The least-squares problem is

$$
\hat{x} = \arg\min_x \|Ax - b\|_2^2.
$$ {#eq-least-squares}

The optimality condition leads to the normal equations

$$
A^\top A \hat{x} = A^\top b.
$$ {#eq-normal-equations}

Equation @eq-normal-equations says the residual is orthogonal to the column space of `A`.

::: {#prp-projection}
## Projection Principle

The least-squares approximation of `b` in the column space of `A` is the orthogonal projection of `b` onto that subspace.
:::

## Worked Example

In linear regression, `A` is the design matrix, `x` is the parameter vector, and `b` is the observed target vector. Least squares chooses the parameter vector whose prediction error is smallest in Euclidean norm.

## Application Lens

This idea shows up in:

- linear regression
- overdetermined systems
- signal approximation
- PCA preprocessing
- inverse problems

## Paper Bridge

Any paper that uses regression, matrix sketching, or linear inverse problems is probably leaning on this page somewhere, even if it never pauses to explain it.
